heroOverview: An acoustic neuroma — more precisely called a vestibular schwannoma — is a benign, slow-growing tumor that arises from Schwann cells of the vestibular division of the eighth cranial nerve, within the internal auditory canal or at the cerebellopontine angle. Acoustic neuromas are diagnosed in approximately 1-2 per 100,000 people per year and account for roughly 8% of all intracranial tumors. Gradual, asymmetric sensorineural hearing loss is the hallmark symptom, occurring in 95% of patients and often dismissed for years. Untreated large tumors compress the brainstem and cerebellum. Three management strategies — surveillance MRI, stereotactic radiosurgery (Gamma Knife), and microsurgical resection — each offer favorable outcomes depending on tumor size and patient preference.

keyStats.prevalence: Incidence approximately 1.2-1.9 per 100,000 per year; estimated 9,000-10,000 new diagnoses annually in the United States (SEER data)
keyStats.demographics: Sporadic tumors affect men and women equally; peak diagnosis age 45-65. Bilateral tumors indicate neurofibromatosis type 2 (NF2), typically presenting before age 30.
keyStats.avgAge: Mean age at diagnosis approximately 50 years for sporadic unilateral tumors
keyStats.globalCases: Prevalence estimated 1 per 1,000 general population based on autopsy studies; most are incidentally detected and asymptomatic

definition: Acoustic neuroma (ICD-10: D33.3; also D14.0 for internal auditory canal location) is a benign intracranial tumor arising from the myelin-forming Schwann cells of the vestibular nerve (superior or inferior division) rather than the auditory nerve, making the term 'acoustic neuroma' a misnomer in strictest sense — vestibular schwannoma is the anatomically precise name. The tumor originates at the Obersteiner-Redlich zone, where central (oligodendrocyte) myelination transitions to peripheral (Schwann cell) myelination at the porus acousticus of the internal auditory canal, usually on the vestibular portion of the eighth cranial nerve. As it grows, it expands medially into the cerebellopontine angle (CPA) cistern, where it can compress the facial nerve (cranial nerve VII), the trigeminal nerve (cranial nerve V), the brainstem, and the cerebellum. Tumors are classified by size: intracanalicular (confined to the internal auditory canal), small (extending into the CPA cistern, up to 15 mm medial extension), medium (15-30 mm), and large (above 30 mm). Approximately 5% of cases are associated with NF2, where bilateral tumors arise from mutations in the NF2 gene encoding merlin. Sporadic acoustic neuromas arise from somatic NF2 mutations or other mechanisms. Annual tumor growth varies widely — roughly 50-60% of monitored tumors grow less than 1 mm per year and up to 35% remain stable or shrink. Neurosurgery, neuro-otology, and radiation oncology share management depending on tumor size, growth, and patient hearing status.

typesClassification.description: Accounts for 95% of cases. Typically presents in the fifth or sixth decade. Caused by somatic NF2 gene mutation or other molecular events in Schwann cells. Managed by surveillance, radiosurgery, or surgery based on size and growth.

Germline mutations in the NF2 tumor suppressor gene on chromosome 22. Diagnostic criterion for neurofibromatosis type 2. Bilateral tumors typically present before age 30. Management is complex due to bilateral hearing loss risk and associated CNS tumors (meningiomas, ependymomas).

Tumor confined entirely within the internal auditory canal. Presents with hearing loss and tinnitus but no brainstem compression. Surveillance or primary radiosurgery is commonly chosen to preserve hearing before cochlear nerve damage is irreversible.

Large tumor with substantial brainstem displacement, obstructive hydrocephalus risk, and facial nerve stretching. Surgical resection is usually recommended, typically via retrosigmoid or translabyrinthine approach depending on hearing preservation intent.

primarySymptoms: Asymmetric sensorineural hearing loss — gradual, progressive, and predominantly affecting high-frequency hearing — present in approximately 95% of patients at diagnosis, often unilateral and initially dismissed as age-related hearing loss.
Tinnitus, typically unilateral and high-pitched, occurring in 60-70% of patients — often the first symptom to prompt medical evaluation when it develops asymmetrically.
Unsteadiness and imbalance from vestibular nerve dysfunction, affecting up to 70% of patients; typically mild and insidious, allowing central compensation over time.
Sudden sensorineural hearing loss (SSNHL) as the presenting event in approximately 10-15% of acoustic neuroma patients — an episode of SSNHL warrants gadolinium-enhanced MRI to exclude a tumor.
Facial numbness or tingling in the V2 or V3 distribution of the trigeminal nerve, indicating large tumor medial extension into the cerebellopontine angle cistern compressing cranial nerve V.
Facial weakness or twitching from facial nerve (cranial nerve VII) compression by large tumors — rarer as an initial symptom but appears in growing lesions.
Headache and nausea from raised intracranial pressure when a large tumor blocks CSF flow at the fourth ventricle, causing obstructive hydrocephalus.
Aural fullness — a sensation of pressure in the ear — reported by approximately 25-30% of patients, distinct from eustachian tube dysfunction.
earlyWarningSigns: Asymmetric hearing loss between ears of more than 15 dB at two or more frequencies on audiometry warrants gadolinium-enhanced MRI of the internal auditory canals.
Unilateral tinnitus — especially if constant, high-pitched, and recently developed — in the absence of obvious otological cause such as noise exposure.
Unilateral vestibular symptoms (recurring unsteadiness without frank vertigo) in a patient over 40 without other ear pathology.
Sudden sensorineural hearing loss on one side — 10-15% of cases are later found to have an acoustic neuroma; MRI is recommended after steroid treatment.
Failed speech discrimination test asymmetry between ears (more than 20% difference) on audiological evaluation.
emergencySigns: Rapid onset of severe headache, vomiting, and ataxia in a known acoustic neuroma patient — suggests obstructive hydrocephalus from cyst expansion or sudden tumor growth requiring urgent neurosurgical evaluation.
Facial paralysis developing acutely in a large acoustic neuroma, suggesting rapid tumor expansion or hemorrhage within the tumor.
Loss of corneal reflex combined with facial numbness and hearing loss, signaling advanced brainstem compression by a large CPA tumor.
Altered consciousness, diplopia, or severe gait ataxia in a patient with a large acoustic neuroma — indicates brainstem compression and requires emergency imaging.

causes.cause: Somatic NF2 gene mutation in Schwann cells
causes.description: Sporadic acoustic neuromas arise from loss of function of both copies of the NF2 tumor suppressor gene, which encodes merlin (schwannomin). Merlin regulates cell proliferation via multiple signaling pathways including mTOR and Rac-1. Loss of merlin permits uncontrolled Schwann cell division, forming the benign tumor.

Individuals with NF2 inherit one defective NF2 allele; a somatic second-hit mutation in the other allele triggers bilateral vestibular schwannoma formation. NF2 follows autosomal dominant inheritance with near-complete penetrance. Associated with meningiomas, ependymomas, and posterior subcapsular cataracts.

causes.cause: Prior low-dose radiation to the head or neck
causes.description: Therapeutic irradiation for childhood cancers (e.g., medulloblastoma, acute leukemia) approximately doubles the risk of developing a schwannoma decades later. The latency period is typically 10-30 years. Radiation-induced schwannomas tend to behave more aggressively than sporadic tumors.

Several epidemiological studies investigated mobile phone use and acoustic neuroma risk. The Interphone study and Danish cohort studies found no convincing increased risk in regular users. WHO IARC classified radiofrequency electromagnetic fields as possibly carcinogenic (Group 2B), but acoustic neuroma risk from phone use remains unproven at current exposure levels.

riskFactors.description: Carriers of germline NF2 mutations have near-certain bilateral acoustic neuroma development, often before age 30. Genetic testing and family screening are essential in any patient presenting with bilateral acoustic neuromas or young-onset unilateral tumor with skin lesions or other NF2 features.
Peak incidence in the fifth and sixth decade for sporadic tumors. Older age at diagnosis is associated with slower growth rates and greater tolerance of surveillance as a management strategy.

riskFactors.factor: Prior therapeutic head and neck irradiation
riskFactors.description: Low-dose ionizing radiation from childhood cancer treatment increases acoustic neuroma risk approximately 2-3 fold. Latency of 10-30 years means survivors of pediatric cancers should have audiological and neurological surveillance.
Parathyroid tumors occur in some NF2 phenotype variants. The association is with specific NF2 truncating mutations rather than sporadic schwannomas.
In NF2-related acoustic neuromas, women may have faster tumor growth rates, possibly linked to estrogen receptor expression on schwannoma cells. Sporadic tumors show equal sex distribution.

affectedDemographics: Sporadic acoustic neuroma: equal sex distribution; predominantly diagnosed in people aged 45-65; all ethnicities affected.
NF2-associated acoustic neuromas: onset typically before age 30; equal sex distribution; autosomal dominant inheritance affects approximately 1 in 25,000-33,000 people.
Children and adolescents: acoustic neuromas are rare under age 20 but when present, NF2 should always be excluded by genetic testing.
Radiation survivors: survivors of childhood CNS malignancies treated with cranial radiation have a measurably increased acoustic neuroma incidence starting 10-20 years post-treatment.
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis begins with audiological evaluation. Asymmetric sensorineural hearing loss — even a subtle high-frequency dip or an unexplained asymmetry in speech discrimination — is the primary trigger for imaging. The American Academy of Otolaryngology-Head and Neck Surgery (AAO-HNS) guidelines recommend gadolinium-enhanced MRI of the internal auditory canals as the gold-standard imaging test; it detects tumors as small as 2-3 mm with near-100% sensitivity and specificity. CT of the skull base is insensitive to small acoustic neuromas but can supplement MRI for bony anatomy and surgical planning. The diagnostic pathway typically involves: (1) clinical history of asymmetric hearing loss, tinnitus, or imbalance; (2) audiogram showing asymmetric sensorineural pattern or abnormal speech discrimination; (3) gadolinium MRI confirming an enhancing tumor in the CPA or internal auditory canal. Auditory brainstem response (ABR) testing was historically used as a screening test but is now largely replaced by MRI due to its superior sensitivity for small intracanalicular tumors. Tumor size, relationship to the facial nerve, and extent of CPA cistern involvement are characterized on MRI to guide treatment planning. For NF2, dedicated gadolinium-enhanced MRI of the entire neuroaxis identifies associated meningiomas, ependymomas, and peripheral nerve sheath tumors. Annual volumetric MRI surveillance (using T1 gadolinium-enhanced sequences with dedicated 1 mm-slice IAC protocols) is the monitoring standard during watchful waiting.

diagnosticTests.test: Gadolinium-enhanced MRI of the internal auditory canals (IAC protocol)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Gold-standard test for detecting acoustic neuroma; identifies tumors as small as 2-3 mm in the internal auditory canal
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 30-45 minutes in an MRI scanner. A small amount of gadolinium contrast is injected via an arm cannula. Acoustic neuromas appear as brightly enhancing masses. The scan is painless; ear plugs are provided for noise. Results typically available within 24-48 hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Documents the degree and pattern of hearing loss; asymmetric sensorineural hearing loss or poor speech discrimination are the primary diagnostic triggers
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 30-minute hearing test in a soundproofed booth. Pure tones at different frequencies are played through headphones; the patient presses a button when they hear a sound. Speech discrimination testing follows. No discomfort involved.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Electrophysiological test of auditory nerve conduction; prolonged interaural wave V latency suggests nerve compression
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Electrodes are placed on the scalp and earlobes; click sounds are played through earphones. Takes 30-45 minutes. Sensitivity approximately 80-90% for acoustic neuromas; largely replaced by MRI in diagnostic workup but still used for intraoperative monitoring.

diagnosticTests.test: CT of the temporal bone (skull base)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Assesses bony enlargement of the internal auditory canal (IAC widening) and surgical anatomy; less sensitive than MRI for soft-tissue tumor characterization
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 5-minute scan; no contrast usually needed for bony assessment. Provides complementary information to MRI for surgical planning but does not detect small intracanalicular tumors.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies germline NF2 mutations in patients with bilateral acoustic neuromas, young-onset unilateral tumors, or associated meningiomas
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A blood draw analyzed in a molecular genetics lab. Turnaround 4-8 weeks. A positive result changes management significantly and triggers family screening.

diagnosticTests.test: Videonystagmography (VNG) and vestibular evoked myogenic potentials (VEMP)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies vestibular nerve function; unilateral canal paresis on caloric testing supports acoustic neuroma diagnosis when MRI is being considered
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Involves wearing video goggles and watching moving targets while warm or cool water or air is instilled in the ear canal. VEMPs are recorded from neck or ocular muscles. Tests take 60-90 minutes. Mild dizziness may occur.

treatmentOverview: Three evidence-based strategies exist: observation with serial MRI, stereotactic radiosurgery (SRS), and microsurgical resection. Choice depends on tumor size, growth rate, existing hearing function, patient age, general health, and preference. Observation (watchful waiting with annual MRI) is appropriate for small or intracanalicular tumors in older patients or those with contralateral hearing loss, because 50-60% of monitored tumors grow less than 1 mm per year and 30-35% remain stable or shrink over 5 years. Stereotactic radiosurgery — delivered as single-session Gamma Knife radiosurgery (GKS) or as fractionated stereotactic radiotherapy (FSRT) — achieves tumor control (prevention of significant growth) in 90-95% of tumors under 3 cm at 5 years. GKS delivers a precisely shaped high-dose radiation field in a single outpatient session; hearing preservation is achieved in 40-70% of cases at 2-year follow-up. GKS is generally the first-line active treatment for small to medium tumors with useful hearing, and for patients not suited to surgery. Microsurgical removal is recommended for large tumors (above 3 cm), tumors causing brainstem compression or hydrocephalus, or when tissue diagnosis is required. Surgical approaches include the translabyrinthine (sacrifices remaining hearing but provides wide CPA access), retrosigmoid (hearing-preservation possible), and middle fossa (best for hearing preservation in small intracanalicular tumors). Facial nerve preservation is paramount: modern microsurgical series report anatomical facial nerve preservation rates of 90-98% for tumors under 2 cm; functional preservation (House-Brackmann grade I-II) rates decline with tumor size. For NF2-associated tumors, auditory brainstem implant (ABI) provides some auditory rehabilitation when both cochlear nerves are sacrificed.

medicalTreatments.name: Observation with serial gadolinium-enhanced MRI
medicalTreatments.description: Active surveillance without intervention. MRI performed at 6 months after diagnosis, then annually for 5 years, then every 2-3 years in stable disease. Appropriate for intracanalicular or small tumors in elderly patients, patients with single hearing ear, and those declining active treatment.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: 30-35% of observed tumors remain stable or shrink; 50-60% grow less than 1 mm/year; fewer than 5% require emergency intervention. Hearing may decline during observation.

medicalTreatments.name: Bevacizumab (Avastin) — for NF2-associated tumors
medicalTreatments.description: Anti-VEGF monoclonal antibody that reduces tumor vascularity in NF2-related schwannomas. Achieves volumetric response (>20% reduction) in approximately 40% of NF2 patients and hearing improvement in a subset. Not used for sporadic acoustic neuromas.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Hearing improvement in 36% and volumetric response in 40% of NF2-acoustic neuroma patients in the largest prospective series (Blakeley et al. 2012); responses are not durable in all patients

medicalTreatments.name: Corticosteroids (prednisone 1 mg/kg/day for 10-14 days) for SSNHL presentation
medicalTreatments.description: When acoustic neuroma presents as sudden sensorineural hearing loss, high-dose oral or intratympanic corticosteroids are given immediately to treat the inflammatory component and attempt hearing recovery before confirmatory MRI results are available.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Hearing recovery from SSNHL achieved in 40-70% of patients treated within 24-72 hours; does not affect tumor biology but may preserve serviceable hearing before definitive treatment

surgicalOptions.name: Gamma Knife stereotactic radiosurgery (single-session SRS)
surgicalOptions.description: Outpatient treatment delivering precisely targeted high-dose radiation to the tumor in a single session using a stereotactic frame. Tumor control (defined as prevention of significant growth) is the goal; the tumor does not disappear but ceases growing. Preferred for tumors under 3 cm with or without serviceable hearing.
surgicalOptions.successRate: 5-year tumor control rate 90-95%; hearing preservation at 50-70% at 2 years in patients with serviceable hearing before treatment; facial nerve preservation exceeds 98%

surgicalOptions.name: Retrosigmoid microsurgery (suboccipital approach)
surgicalOptions.description: Posterior fossa craniotomy providing access to both the cerebellopontine angle and the internal auditory canal. Allows hearing preservation attempts in patients with useful hearing and tumors up to 2.5 cm. Provides the most direct route to the CPA and is the most versatile surgical approach.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Hearing preservation approximately 40-60% for small tumors; total tumor removal 85-95%; facial nerve functional preservation (HB grade I-II) 90-95% for tumors under 2 cm

surgicalOptions.name: Translabyrinthine microsurgery
surgicalOptions.description: Approach through the mastoid and inner ear that sacrifices residual hearing but provides wide exposure of the internal auditory canal and CPA without cerebellar retraction. Preferred when hearing is already non-functional and for very large tumors.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Total tumor removal in 90-95%; facial nerve anatomical preservation 98%; no hearing preservation possible; lower rate of cerebellar retraction complications

surgicalOptions.name: Middle fossa craniotomy
surgicalOptions.description: Temporal craniotomy providing direct access to the internal auditory canal from above. Best approach for small intracanalicular tumors when hearing preservation is the priority.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Hearing preservation in 50-65% of selected patients with small intracanalicular tumors; total removal rate 85-95%; technically demanding approach with limited CPA exposure

surgicalOptions.name: Fractionated stereotactic radiotherapy (FSRT)
surgicalOptions.description: Radiotherapy delivered in 25-30 fractions over 5-6 weeks, used when the tumor is too large or too close to critical structures for single-session GKS. Provides similar tumor control to GKS with potentially better tolerance in large tumors.
surgicalOptions.successRate: 5-year tumor control rate approximately 88-94% for tumors 2-4 cm; comparable to GKS for similar size tumors in non-randomized comparisons

whySeeSpecialist: All suspected acoustic neuromas require referral to a neurotology or neurosurgery center experienced in CPA tumor management. Treatment decisions — surgery versus Gamma Knife versus observation — involve detailed multidisciplinary assessment of audiological function, tumor size, growth rate, and facial nerve risk. Sub-specialty expertise significantly affects hearing and facial nerve preservation outcomes.
doctorSelectionGuide: Choose a center performing more than 50 acoustic neuroma surgeries per year — outcome data show clear volume-outcome relationships for facial nerve preservation and hearing preservation rates. A dedicated cerebellopontine angle multidisciplinary team including neurosurgeon, neurotologist, and radiation oncologist is the international standard of care. The Acoustic Neuroma Association maintains a US center directory.
hospitalCriteria: Gamma Knife or dedicated linear accelerator radiosurgery facility
High-volume neurosurgery center with CPA surgery expertise
Intraoperative neurophysiology monitoring for facial and cochlear nerves
Neurotology service for audiological evaluation and cochlear implant assessment
Dedicated neuro-MRI protocol for internal auditory canal imaging
keyFacilities: Acoustic neuroma centers of excellence (Acoustic Neuroma Association directory)
Academic neurosurgery programs with dedicated CPA teams
Neuro-otology clinics for NF2 management and auditory rehabilitation
preventionStrategies: There are no proven lifestyle-based prevention strategies for sporadic acoustic neuroma. Avoid unnecessary radiation to the head and neck in childhood, which increases schwannoma risk.
In NF2 families, genetic counseling and germline testing of first-degree relatives allows early tumor detection through surveillance MRI before hearing loss becomes severe.
Screen patients with NF2 annually with dedicated IAC MRI from early adolescence to detect tumors before they grow large enough to require surgery.
lifestyleModifications: During observation, attend serial MRI appointments without fail — tumor growth triggering urgent intervention is most safely caught on scheduled surveillance.
Protect hearing in the better-hearing ear by using ear protection in high-noise environments; avoid ototoxic medications when alternatives exist.
Communicate hearing loss to employers and healthcare providers to arrange accommodations and assistive listening devices.
Balance and vestibular rehabilitation exercises help the brain compensate for unilateral vestibular deficits, reducing fall risk.
Join a patient support network — the Acoustic Neuroma Association reports significant improvements in treatment decision quality and patient satisfaction when peer support is available.

recommended: Omega-3-rich foods (fatty fish, walnuts, flaxseed) for general neural health during recovery
High-protein diet supporting wound healing in the post-surgical period
Adequate hydration to prevent post-surgical nausea and headache
Antioxidant-rich fruits and vegetables for general health during radiation treatment
avoid: Ototoxic medications (aminoglycoside antibiotics, high-dose loop diuretics) that damage hearing when alternatives exist
High-noise environments without hearing protection, which accelerate hearing loss in the affected ear
Alcohol and sedatives in the immediate post-operative or post-radiosurgery period when vestibular adaptation is underway

exerciseGuidelines: Vestibular rehabilitation therapy — supervised exercises targeting gaze stability, balance, and gait — is recommended for all patients with unilateral vestibular deficits. Begin within 2-4 weeks of diagnosis or post-treatment. Swimming and cycling on stationary bikes are safe during vestibular compensation. Contact sports and high-fall-risk activities should be avoided until vestibular balance is fully compensated.
dailyManagement: Attend scheduled surveillance MRI appointments; do not defer or miss scans.
Use a written hearing-loss log to track changes in hearing, tinnitus pitch or volume, or balance — report deterioration promptly.
Perform daily vestibular rehabilitation exercises (gaze stabilization, Cawthorne-Cooksey exercises) as prescribed.
Inform other healthcare providers about the diagnosis and any active treatment; many drugs have ototoxic potential.
After Gamma Knife treatment, monitor for early radiosurgery effects: facial numbness or headache at 3-18 months is common and usually resolves.
prognosis: Acoustic neuromas are benign, with no capacity for distant metastasis or malignant transformation under standard conditions. The primary morbidities are hearing loss and facial nerve dysfunction. For Gamma Knife radiosurgery, 5-year tumor control rates of 90-95% are consistently reported across large series; 10-year rates remain above 88%. Hearing is preserved in 40-70% at 2 years after radiosurgery. Microsurgical outcomes for facial nerve depend heavily on tumor size: functional preservation (House-Brackmann grade I-II) approaches 95% for tumors under 2 cm but falls to 70-80% for tumors exceeding 3 cm in experienced hands. NF2-associated acoustic neuromas carry a more guarded prognosis due to bilateral hearing loss and associated CNS tumors. Malignant transformation of acoustic neuroma is extremely rare and almost exclusively reported after prior radiotherapy. Overall life expectancy is not reduced by sporadic acoustic neuroma.
recoveryTimeline: After Gamma Knife radiosurgery, no hospitalization is required; mild fatigue and headache resolve in 1-2 weeks. Tumor response on MRI (swelling then stabilization) is assessed at 6 months and annually. After microsurgery, hospital stay is 2-5 days; vestibular adaptation takes 6-12 weeks; hearing and facial nerve recovery, if expected, may continue for up to 12 months. Facial nerve recovery after paresis can take 6-18 months.
complications: Ipsilateral sensorineural hearing loss — occurs in 30-60% of patients after radiosurgery and in nearly all after translabyrinthine surgery; cochlear implant may restore hearing after cochlear nerve preservation.
Facial nerve weakness (House-Brackmann grade III or higher) after surgery — risk increases with tumor size; occurs in 2-10% for tumors under 2 cm in experienced centers.
Tinnitus worsening — reported in 10-20% of patients post-radiosurgery; typically self-limiting over 6-12 months.

Hydrocephalus from large tumor obstructing CSF pathways — presents with headache, cognitive slowing, and gait disturbance; requires CSF diversion (ventriculoperitoneal shunt or endoscopic third ventriculostomy).
Cerebrospinal fluid leak after microsurgery — occurs in 5-10%; most managed conservatively with bed rest; persistent leaks require lumbar drain or surgical repair.
Radiation-induced cranial nerve neuropathy or secondary malignancy — very rare; reported decades after initial irradiation.

supportResources.name: Acoustic Neuroma Association (ANA)
supportResources.description: US patient organization providing treatment center directories, peer support, and educational resources on vestibular schwannoma management.

supportResources.name: British Acoustic Neuroma Association (BANA)
supportResources.description: UK patient support charity for acoustic neuroma with helpline, treatment information, and peer network.

supportResources.name: Children's Tumor Foundation — NF2
supportResources.description: Guidance for patients and families with NF2, including clinical trial listings and genetic counseling resources.

confusedWithConditions.name: Meningioma
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Cerebellopontine angle meningiomas can mimic acoustic neuromas with similar location and hearing symptoms. Meningiomas typically arise from the posterior petrous face rather than the internal auditory canal, show no internal auditory canal widening on CT, and have a broad dural tail on MRI. Tissue diagnosis by biopsy or resection is required for definitive distinction.

confusedWithConditions.name: Meniere Disease
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Meniere disease causes episodic vertigo, fluctuating low-frequency hearing loss, aural fullness, and tinnitus. Acoustic neuroma produces slowly progressive unilateral hearing loss without episodic vertigo; audiogram shows high-frequency sensorineural loss. Gadolinium MRI is normal in Meniere disease, whereas acoustic neuroma shows an enhancing CPA or IAC mass.

confusedWithConditions.name: Sensorineural Hearing Loss (Age-related)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Age-related (presbycusis) hearing loss is bilateral, symmetric, and high-frequency without an identifiable structural cause. Acoustic neuroma produces asymmetric hearing loss with abnormal speech discrimination disproportionate to pure-tone thresholds; MRI differentiates the two. Any asymmetric hearing loss of more than 15 dB at two or more frequencies warrants MRI.

relatedConditions.name: Meningioma
relatedConditions.relevance: Both are benign intracranial tumors managed at CPA surgery centers; meningiomas can arise in the same anatomical region and share radiosurgery as a treatment modality.

relatedConditions.name: Meniere Disease
relatedConditions.relevance: Primary differential diagnosis for unilateral hearing loss and vestibular symptoms; distinguished by MRI and audiological pattern.

relatedConditions.name: Tinnitus
relatedConditions.relevance: Unilateral tinnitus is a key early symptom of acoustic neuroma; all patients with asymmetric tinnitus should have acoustic neuroma excluded by MRI.

relatedConditions.name: Brain Tumor (Glioma)
relatedConditions.relevance: Both are treated at neuro-oncology and neurosurgery centers; NF2 patients have concurrent CNS tumor risk including ependymomas.

faqs.question: What is an acoustic neuroma?
faqs.answer: An acoustic neuroma (vestibular schwannoma) is a benign, slow-growing tumor of the eighth cranial nerve, arising from Schwann cells of the vestibular division. It grows in the internal auditory canal and cerebellopontine angle, causing asymmetric hearing loss, tinnitus, and imbalance. It accounts for about 8% of all brain tumors.

faqs.question: What are the first signs of an acoustic neuroma?
faqs.answer: The first sign is almost always asymmetric sensorineural hearing loss, affecting one ear more than the other. This occurs in 95% of patients and may be gradual or sudden. Unilateral tinnitus and mild unsteadiness are other early symptoms. Sudden hearing loss in one ear warrants MRI to exclude an acoustic neuroma.

faqs.question: Is an acoustic neuroma cancer?
faqs.answer: No. Acoustic neuromas are benign tumors with no ability to spread to other parts of the body. However, they can cause significant harm if untreated by growing large enough to compress the facial nerve, brainstem, or cerebellum. Malignant transformation is extremely rare.

faqs.question: How is an acoustic neuroma diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Gadolinium-enhanced MRI of the internal auditory canals is the gold-standard test, detecting tumors as small as 2-3 mm. The diagnostic pathway begins with an audiogram showing asymmetric sensorineural hearing loss, which triggers MRI. CT of the temporal bone provides complementary bony anatomy but cannot detect small tumors.

faqs.question: What is the best treatment for an acoustic neuroma?
faqs.answer: Treatment depends on tumor size and patient factors. Small tumors in older patients are often observed with annual MRI. Gamma Knife radiosurgery controls tumor growth in 90-95% of cases and is preferred for small-to-medium tumors. Microsurgical removal is recommended for large tumors causing brainstem compression or hydrocephalus.

faqs.question: Can acoustic neuroma cause permanent hearing loss?
faqs.answer: Yes. Most patients have some hearing loss at diagnosis, and it can worsen with tumor growth, radiosurgery, or surgery. Hearing preservation is possible in 40-70% after Gamma Knife and 40-65% after hearing-preservation microsurgery for small tumors. Translabyrinthine surgery sacrifices residual hearing. Cochlear implantation may restore hearing after cochlear nerve preservation.

faqs.question: What is Gamma Knife treatment for acoustic neuroma?
faqs.answer: Gamma Knife stereotactic radiosurgery delivers precisely targeted high-dose radiation to the tumor in a single outpatient session. It stops tumor growth in 90-95% of tumors under 3 cm at 5 years. Hearing is preserved in 40-70% of patients with useful hearing at treatment. No incision or general anaesthesia is required; the procedure takes 1-2 hours.

faqs.question: Do acoustic neuromas always need surgery?
faqs.answer: No. About 30-35% of small tumors remain stable or shrink during observation. Gamma Knife radiosurgery is a non-surgical alternative with 90-95% tumor control rates. Surgery is recommended mainly for large tumors (above 3 cm), tumors compressing the brainstem, rapidly growing lesions, or when tissue diagnosis is needed.

faqs.question: Can an acoustic neuroma cause facial paralysis?
faqs.answer: Spontaneous facial paralysis from an acoustic neuroma is uncommon and suggests a large tumor compressing the facial nerve. After surgery, temporary or permanent facial weakness can occur; the risk increases with tumor size. In experienced centers, functional facial nerve preservation exceeds 95% for tumors under 2 cm.

faqs.question: What causes acoustic neuromas?
faqs.answer: Sporadic acoustic neuromas arise from somatic mutations in the NF2 tumor suppressor gene in Schwann cells. NF2-related bilateral acoustic neuromas result from germline NF2 mutations. Prior head or neck irradiation in childhood doubles the risk. Most sporadic tumors have no identifiable environmental cause.

faqs.question: How fast does an acoustic neuroma grow?
faqs.answer: Growth rates vary widely. About 30-35% of monitored tumors remain stable or shrink over 5 years. Roughly 50-60% grow less than 1 mm per year. A small proportion grows rapidly at more than 2-3 mm per year and requires treatment. Annual MRI during observation quantifies growth reliably.

faqs.question: Can acoustic neuroma affect balance permanently?
faqs.answer: Unsteadiness from unilateral vestibular nerve damage usually improves over 3-12 months as the brain compensates through a process called vestibular adaptation. Vestibular rehabilitation therapy accelerates recovery. Persistent balance impairment is uncommon for patients treated at experienced centers using modern microsurgical or radiosurgical techniques.

faqs.question: Is acoustic neuroma hereditary?
faqs.answer: Sporadic acoustic neuromas are not inherited. Bilateral acoustic neuromas indicate neurofibromatosis type 2 (NF2), which is autosomal dominant — each child of an affected parent has a 50% chance of inheriting the NF2 mutation. Genetic counseling is essential for NF2 families, and first-degree relatives should be screened.

faqs.question: What follow-up is needed after acoustic neuroma treatment?
faqs.answer: After Gamma Knife, MRI is performed at 6 months then annually for 5 years, then every 2-3 years in stable disease. After surgery, MRI at 1 year confirms complete resection; residual tumor is monitored annually. Audiological and facial nerve function assessments are performed at each visit. Long-term surveillance is recommended as late recurrence or regrowth can occur.

faqs.question: Can a person with one acoustic neuroma develop another?
faqs.answer: Recurrence in the same ear after complete resection occurs in under 5% of cases at 10 years. A new acoustic neuroma in the opposite ear in a patient without NF2 is extremely rare. However, patients with NF2 develop bilateral acoustic neuromas as a hallmark feature. Any new asymmetric hearing change after treatment warrants MRI.

metaTitle: Acoustic Neuroma: Symptoms, Diagnosis & Treatment | aihealz
metaDescription: Acoustic neuroma: a benign tumor on the hearing and balance nerve causing hearing loss. Symptoms, MRI diagnosis, Gamma Knife and surgery options reviewed here.

sources.title: Stangerup SE, Caye-Thomasen P. 2012. Epidemiology and natural history of vestibular schwannomas. Otolaryngol Clin North Am 45(2):257-68.
Koos WT, Day JD, Matula C, Levy DI. 1998. Neurotopographic considerations in the microsurgical treatment of small acoustic neurinomas. J Neurosurg 88(3):506-12.
